questions.question: Which statement about Java packages vs. C# namespaces is correct?
choices: Both are pure logical groupings; folder structure is convention-only in both languages.
A Java package must match the source file's directory path under src/main/java; the compiler enforces this. C# does not.
C# enforces folder/namespace matching; Java does not.
Java packages are a runtime concept only; folder layout is irrelevant.
questions.explanation: Java couples package declaration to directory path under src/main/java - javac rejects mismatches and classloading depends on the agreement. C# treats namespaces as logical groupings with no compiler-enforced folder mapping.

questions.question: What does the default (no keyword) access modifier on a Java class member mean?
choices: Private - accessible only inside the declaring class.
Package-private - accessible to other classes in the same package, invisible outside.
questions.explanation: Java's default access is package-private: visible within the declaring package but not outside. It's roughly equivalent to C#'s internal, but scoped per-package rather than per-assembly.

questions.question: Your code calls getClass().getResourceAsStream("/config.yml") and it returns null at runtime, but the file exists in src/main/resources/config.yml. Which is the most likely cause?
choices: Resources cannot be loaded after packaging into a JAR; only File I/O works.
The file was not packaged onto the classpath (e.g. wrong source set, custom build excluding it, or the working directory was confused with the classpath).
Leading slashes are illegal in getResourceAsStream paths.
Spring Boot disables classpath loading by default.
questions.explanation: Resources are looked up on the classpath, not by file path. Returning null typically means the file wasn't included in the build output (target/classes or the JAR). Verify with `jar tf` and confirm src/main/resources is part of the active source set.
